region_of_origin: Levant (Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, Palestine); widely adopted across Middle Eastern and Mediterranean cuisines

ontology.foodex2_description: Aubergine/eggplant-based dip or spread (roasted, blended with tahini and seasonings)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for baba ganoush; A0CKP (eggplant/aubergine product) used as closest defensible parent anchor. Tahini-based dip category not separately coded in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for baba ganoush as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual prepared food dishes at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of baba ganoush as a dip or mezze portion is approximately 200 g (roughly 4–6 tablespoons), as commonly served in restaurant or takeaway contexts in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Baba Ghanoush, SR Legacy FDC ID 173944
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA), Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Baba ghanoush' (FDC ID 173944) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values for energy (175 kcal), protein (4 g), fat (15 g), carbohydrate (7.5 g), dietary fibre (4 g), sodium (200 mg), saturated fat (2 g), sugar (1.5 g), calcium (50 mg), iron (1 mg), cholesterol (0 mg), trans fat (0 g), and vitamin C (7.5 mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy composite data for this dish type and are retained from the input record with USDA cross-reference support. Monounsaturated fat (8.5 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated fat (3.5 g/100 g) estimated from USDA fatty acid profiles for olive oil and tahini at typical recipe proportions. Potassium (230 mg/100 g) estimated from USDA component data for roasted eggplant and tahini.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for baba ganoush exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. GI estimated at approximately 15 (low) based on: (1) roasted eggplant GI ~15 per University of Sydney GI Database analogous vegetable entry; (2) tahini (sesame paste) GI ~35 (low) per University of Sydney GI Database; (3) olive oil and lemon juice contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not raise GI. The composite dish has very low carbohydrate (7.5 g/100 g) and high fat and fibre content, which further attenuates glycemic response. GL per 200 g serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (15 × 15.0) / 100 = 2.25, rounded to 2.3. This is a low-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 200 mg/100 g is at the borderline threshold; offset by potassium (~230 mg/100 g) and high dietary fibre (4 g/100 g) from eggplant and tahini; olive oil provides heart-healthy monounsaturated fats.
hypertension.guidance: Baba ganoush is generally suitable for hypertensive individuals at moderate portions (100–150 g); request reduced added salt during preparation to keep sodium below 150 mg/100 g, and avoid pairing with high-sodium accompaniments such as salted crackers or processed bread.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (7.5 g/100 g), low sugar (1.5 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (4 g/100 g), and estimated low GI (~15) combine to produce a very low glycemic load (GL ~2.3 per 200 g serving), making this a diabetes-friendly dip.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Baba ganoush is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI, high fibre, and dominant monounsaturated fat profile from olive oil and tahini; pair with low-GI vegetables or wholegrain bread rather than white pita to maintain overall meal glycemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 2 g/100 g; the dominant fats are monounsaturated (olive oil, ~8.5 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated (tahini, ~3.5 g/100 g), which are cardioprotective; cholesterol is 0 mg; dietary fibre (4 g/100 g) supports LDL reduction.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Baba ganoush is a heart-healthy dip for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease; the high MUFA content from olive oil and sesame-derived PUFA from tahini support favourable lipid profiles, and the dish should be consumed as part of a Mediterranean-style dietary pattern for maximum cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Eggplant is a nightshade vegetable containing solanine alkaloids, which may trigger or worsen joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; smoked paprika is also a nightshade derivative.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and known nightshade sensitivity should avoid baba ganoush due to its eggplant and smoked paprika content; those without nightshade sensitivity may benefit from the anti-inflammatory oleocanthal in olive oil and antioxidant polyphenols from garlic, lemon, and parsley.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baba ganoush (also spelled baba ghanoush, baba ghanouj, or baba ghannoug) is a classic Levantine dip and mezze dish originating from the Middle East, particularly Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, and Palestine, and widely consumed across the Mediterranean and Middle Eastern world. It is prepared by roasting whole eggplants (aubergines) directly over an open flame or under a grill until the skin is charred and the flesh is soft and smoky, then mashing or blending the flesh with tahini (sesame paste), olive oil, fresh lemon juice, garlic, and salt. Variations include ground cumin, smoked paprika, and fresh parsley as garnish. The result is a smooth, creamy, and intensely smoky dip with a rich, nutty flavour from the tahini and a bright acidity from the lemon juice. Nutritionally, baba ganoush is a nutrient-dense, low-carbohydrate dip providing approximately 175 kcal per 100 g, with 15 g of predominantly heart-healthy monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats from olive oil and tahini, 4 g of protein, 7.5 g of carbohydrate, 4 g of dietary fibre, and 200 mg of sodium. It is naturally vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, halal, and dairy-free. The estimated glycemic index is low (~15), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The high fibre and MUFA content support cardiovascular health and blood glucose regulation. The primary allergen is sesame (tahini). In Singapore, baba ganoush is commonly served in Turkish, Lebanese, and Middle Eastern restaurants as a mezze dip alongside pita bread, falafel, and shawarma platters.

Is baba ganoush the same as moutabal?

confidence_reason: No exact baba ganoush entry exists in the Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for baba ghanoush (FDC ID 173944) and cross-referenced with USDA component data for roasted eggplant, tahini, and olive oil. The input ai_generated values were found to be within plausible range and are retained with USDA cross-reference support. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component data. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides an exact match for this specific preparation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173944, baba ghanoush) and USDA component data for roasted eggplant, tahini, and olive oil. Input ai_generated values (provenance: grabfood) were cross-referenced and found consistent with USDA data; retained with USDA as the approved source basis. 2. Monounsaturated fat (8.5 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated fat (3.5 g/100 g) estimated from USDA fatty acid profiles for olive oil and tahini at typical recipe proportions; documented as adapted values. 3. Potassium (230 mg/100 g) estimated from USDA component data for roasted eggplant (~229 mg/100 g) and tahini contribution. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient breakdown for this specific baba ganoush preparation is not fully reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry FDC ID 173944 or Singapore HPB database as of 2026; follow-up with USDA Foundation Foods detailed entry recommended. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg set to 0.0: confirmed zero — fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; no vitamin B12 source present. 6. vitamin_d_mcg set to 0.0: confirmed zero — no vitamin D-containing ingredients; plant-based preparation with no fortification. 7. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used; olive oil and tahini are naturally trans-fat-free. 8. cholesterol_mg set to 0.0: confirmed zero — fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 9. GI estimated at ~15 (low) using analogous component data from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for baba ganoush exists in the database as of 2026. 10. Cultural note: the input record lists primary_cuisine as 'Turkish' and country_of_origin as 'Singapore'; this reflects the Singapore restaurant context (Turkish/Middle Eastern restaurant on GrabFood). The dish's true culinary origin is Levantine (Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, Palestine); this has been corrected in the output. 11. FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as closest defensible parent anchor for eggplant/aubergine product; no exact FoodEx2 code for baba ganoush exists as of 2024; foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent'. 12. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
